text: The robotic-assisted thymectomy begins with the placement of three ports on the left side: two 8mm ports and one 12mm port. An additional 8mm port is sometimes used on the right side to confirm the position of the phrenic nerve. The surgeon starts dissection medial to the left phrenic nerve, moving superiorly to expose the left innominate vein. The left thymic horn is pulled down until reaching the inferior pole of the thyroid, where the vein is clipped off.

The surgeon mobilizes the left thymic lobe off the precordium, following the innominate vein to reach the right thymic horn. The right thymic horn is drawn into the chest, clipped, and diathermy is applied. The procedure aims to remove all thymic tissue between both phrenic nerves and from the thyroid to the diaphragm to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon approaches the start of the superior vena cava (SVC), focusing on the tributaries of the thymic veins and arteries as they connect to the internal mammary vein and artery. The right pleura is opened to enhance exposure and facilitate retraction, highlighting potential sites for bleeding.

The surgeon releases the thymus from the inferior portion above the diaphragm, focusing on the most inferior part of the right thymic lobe. The thymus is moved en bloc into the left pleura, and a retrieval bag is introduced through the twelve millimeter port, visible at the edge of the port.
The innominate vein and superior vena cava (SVC) are visibly denuded of thymic tissue, with clear exposure from one phrenic nerve to the other.
